title: Commentary on Key Financial Performance – December 31, 2026
content:
Capital and Solvency
The credit union maintained a net worth ratio of 10.33%, reflecting an increase of 4 basis points from the prior quarter. The solvency evaluation remained at 1.10. This level of capital provides a substantial buffer against potential losses, ensuring continued financial stability.
Asset Quality
Delinquency stood at 0.34% and net charge-offs were recorded at 0.22%. Both metrics showed improvement compared to the prior quarter, with delinquency rising by 10 basis points but remaining within acceptable parameters while charge-offs declined significantly by 137 basis points. Continued monitoring of asset quality will be important as these improvements suggest effective risk management.
Profitability
Return on average assets (ROAA) was 0.73%, up from the prior period, supported by a net interest margin of 3.35% that also improved quarter-over-quarter. Net operating expenses represented 3.06% of average assets and decreased slightly compared to last quarter. The profitability picture for this period is positive, driven by both yield expansion and expense control.
Funding & Growth
The loans-to-shares ratio was 78.75%, indicating a balanced balance-sheet positioning between lending activity and member funding sources. Loan growth reached 5.47% while share growth totaled 4.99%. The core funding ratio stood at 24.68%. These figures reflect steady expansion in both assets and liabilities, with the institution maintaining its focus on organic membership-driven growth.
Closing
Across all four areas — capital strength, asset quality, profitability, and balanced funding — the credit union presents a mixed but generally resilient picture this period. Capital remains robust to absorb potential shocks; asset quality has improved despite minor upticks in delinquency; profitability benefits from margin expansion and expense discipline; and growth is steady across loans and shares. Continued monitoring of loan-to-share dynamics will be important as balance-sheet positioning evolves, ensuring that funding sources keep pace with lending needs without compromising liquidity or capital adequacy.
